base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2402.10198v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2402.10198v3","title":"SAMformer: Unlocking the Potential of Transformers in Time Series
  Forecasting with Sharpness-Aware Minimization and Channel-Wise Attention","authors":["Romain Ilbert","Ambroise Odonnat","Vasilii Feofanov","Aladin Virmaux","Giuseppe Paolo","Themis Palpanas","Ievgen Redko"],"published_date":"2024-02-15T18:55:05Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Transformer-based architectures achieved breakthrough performance in natural
language processing and computer vision, yet they remain inferior to simpler
linear baselines in multivariate long-term forecasting. To better understand
this phenomenon, we start by studying a toy linear forecasting problem for
which we show that transformers are incapable of converging to their true
solution despite their high expressive power. We further identify the attention
of transformers as being responsible for this low generalization capacity.
Building upon this insight, we propose a shallow lightweight transformer model
that successfully escapes bad local minima when optimized with sharpness-aware
optimization. We empirically demonstrate that this result extends to all
commonly used real-world multivariate time series datasets. In particular,
SAMformer surpasses current state-of-the-art methods and is on par with the
biggest foundation model MOIRAI while having significantly fewer parameters.
The code is available at https://github.com/romilbert/samformer.","github_url":"https://github.com/romilbert/samformer","main_contributions":"The paper addresses the issue of poor generalization of transformer-based architectures in multivariate long-term time series forecasting. It identifies attention mechanisms as a key factor and proposes SAMformer, a shallow transformer model optimized with sharpness-aware minimization (SAM), achieving state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters.","methodology":"The methodology involves analyzing the loss landscape of transformers, identifying entropy collapse and sharpness as issues. The proposed SAMformer model incorporates reversible instance normalization (RevIN) and channel-wise attention, optimized using sharpness-aware minimization (SAM).","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted on 8 publicly available datasets: ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, ETTm2, Electricity, Exchange, Traffic, and Weather. The models were evaluated using MSE and MAE, with a look-back window of 512 and prediction horizons of 96, 192, 336, and 720.","limitations":"The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations but it can be inferred that the model's performance might be sensitive to the choice of the neighborhood size ρ in SAM, as indicated by the sensitivity analysis. The reliance on channel-wise attention may also limit its applicability to datasets where temporal dependencies are more critical.","future_research_directions":"The paper suggests future work could build upon the SAMformer architecture to improve it further, potentially by exploring different attention mechanisms or incorporating other techniques to stabilize training and improve generalization. Exploring the application of SAMformer to other time series forecasting tasks or datasets could also be a direction for future research."}
